data.title: Why Tesla Is A High-Stakes Gamble Now
data.description:
Over the past few years, particularly in the run-up to the U.S. Presidential elections and in its early days, there has hardly been a more polarizing and emotionally charged ticker as Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA). Analysis for TSLA cannot be complete on the basis of numbers alone. Sentiment and brand image play a significant role for any investor riding TSLA's literally electric journey. We look into Tesla as a whole, beyond numbers, to sense where it is headed.

First Up - What We Can Measure

The first impressions we get is of are declining and threatened sales – just look up Tesla sales, and the amount of negative shock news that jump up is alarming. Tesla Sales is primarily dictated by the U.S. and China. Europe (led by Germany, UK and France) is the next significant market. Followed by Australia and Canada. The sales have not been enthusing in the primary market in the U.S., with stagnation and drops evident since 2024. The market share of Tesla also holds up a similar story, and across markets.

Now for international markets, both China and Europe show plunging or stagnating sales. Whichever way you look at the data, what is certainly not evident is a growth that justifies Tesla's mind-boggling valuation.

Among new markets and segments, the biggest upcoming move is into the Indian EV space. Indian EV passenger vehicle sales is currently clocking around 100 thousand units a year. How much Tesla will be able to penetrate the market will depend on the positioning and pricing, as well as the support charging network that is finally put in place. However, the Indian EV market will, at some point, also be more open to competition from Chinese EV automakers (in addition to the already robust domestic players' presence). Even if the Indian market works out like China for Tesla, a market share of 10% makes the potential sales to start at 10K units per year – nothing significant, but with a promise of potential rapid gains, as India rushes towards electric vehicles quickly over time.

The international markets is where Tesla is betting on, and that is where the ground becomes shaky and uncertain after the recent tariff regime giving rise to trade war concerns. China has clearly shown intent to retaliate with reciprocal tariffs. Chinese authorities may delay regulatory approval for Tesla's Full Self-Drive, or FSD, Technology and take several other soft measures to counter the tariff war by making it difficult for Tesla to penetrate further in China.

Apart from authorities, consumer resistance is perceivably rising, and brand sentiment is taking a hit. According to a survey, 94% of Germans now don't want to own a Tesla, likely due to CEO Elon Musk's political engagements. The sentiment is more in the mire now, as the Tariff war will potentially dampen Tesla brand positioning further. Brand sentiment is a difficult variable to predict.

All I am saying is that the current situation lends a "risk" hand to Tesla's image prospects (already Musk's association with conservative politics is at logger heads with the left-wing adoption of EVs – "generally" speaking). The details of how the trade war or tariff details are negotiated with each country going forward, may place Tesla in a favorable position tomorrow, but as of today, the risk is obviously apparent and the Rewards are but a gamble/speculation.

Tesla Valuation Discussion

I typically don't look at Tesla's financials as long as the sales live up to the growth promise. That is lagging as of now. This is where I will draw flak from a lot of fundamental analysts and readers, but in my view, Tesla's valuation is from the cult brand following that came from its first mover advantage in the segment as much as the fact that it is viewed and valued like a Tech company more than a traditional auto company. However, the benefits of path breaking growth levers (such as software, hardware and battery technology ownership in house) should show some traction in the footprint or at least green shoots in the financials.

As of date, Tesla has not yet shown the kind of leadership position it is primed to achieve or opened up new revenue streams effectively either. Till such time that the promises start showing up, much of the valuation remains speculative. Peer or fundamental comparisons are not particularly rewarding.

New potential areas such as autonomous driving, AI and Robotics are very well within Tesla's reach and capability, and those developments could open up opportunities justifying the valuation multiples. However, there should be enough competition, particularly from Chinese EV makers, futuristic tech companies and clean energy initiatives to keep Tesla in check. Regulatory hurdles are additional obstacles in Tesla's uncertain path of global dominance. If the valuation is looking out 5-10 years in the future, then usually, as an investor, I see share prices starting to come closer to earnings per share over time. Even an Amazon trades today at a forward multiple of 30 P/E. To match a similar performance, earnings have to increase at least four-fold for Tesla. This journey will give investors plenty of scope to time another entry as and when the promise to realization story unfolds.

How Is The Competition Landscape?

In addition to traditional carmakers foraying into the EV space rapidly, several Chinese EV makers have started to make bold moves in the segment – BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), XPeng (XPEV), Li Auto (LI), and NIO Inc. (NIO). Although Tesla is the first mover, giant technical strides made by the Chinese EV makers, definitely rob any moat potential for Tesla. In fact, BYD stands tall as per 2024 EV sales stats in China. Several other players are seen close to Tesla in terms of footprint. BYD, in fact, seems to have leapfrogged Tesla in terms of international ambitions as well, with significant growth plans at good pace for Europe, Australia and even entering India before Tesla.

Technical Challenges

The growth of Chinese EV is not without its technical advancements and advantages. Chinese EV makers have been more agile in the past couple of years than Tesla, rolling out newer features, more value with each release at a very fast pace. Tesla's battery efficiency is arguably far better so far than the Chinese competition. However, the charging network and real-world range of batteries (very important in new markets these EVs enter) is something where Tesla lags behind. Zeekr (ZK) and BYD have a 20% larger range on average than Tesla (with bigger batteries, not efficiency). XPeng and BYD also provide better regeneration capabilities for the batteries, which suits city driving. Tesla does better on highways as regeneration is less important here, but still loses out or is matched by the bigger batteries of the Chinese counterparts.

The value proposition (excluding brand image and ownership affinity) is clear when we compare the base Tesla 3 Model with an XPeng P7 or BYD Han. The latter comes with Lidar sensors, gives an overall better range (for a larger battery), more interior space and features. All this for a similar price range (in fact, BYD has come up with even more competitive prices in international markets of late). Users with different requirements and budgets and brand affiliations will debate on who wins, but one thing is clear -Tesla's tech advantage does not deliver a moat advantage.

Tariff Impact

The buzzing topic. We may not have heard the last of this. There will be negotiations and trade deals happening over the next months that could change landscapes. But certainly, uncertainty and medium term damage is already in play. Not to mention, lack of policy stability and predictability will hurt global trade and lead to market shocks. Elon Musk has in the past spoken against Tariffs as means of protectionism and has advocated Open Markets. In some cases, tariffs on importing Tesla were unfair, and the new regime will give a fair playing field for Tesla. But what about the brand damage because of Musk's close association with the U.S. administration!

In China, Tesla already has tough competition from local manufacturers. A significant market risk is seen here as China retaliates and consumers show negative sentiment in the coming days. Supply Chain disruptions are also imminent as several parts for manufacturing are reliant on China. Tesla might be forced to alter the supply chain or manufacturing facilities, leading to inefficiencies in the short term. China manufactured Tesla cars' exports to Europe may also be hit if Europe ramps up further with tariffs, although that is not a direct offshoot of the recent tariff announcements in the U.S. Rising costs and sale prices in some markets due to tariffs, could either lead to sales loss or margin pressure if discounting comes into play to stay competitive. Tesla is placed better than traditional carmakers because of the vertical integration structure and relatively more localized production approach, but the tariff impact is not minimal in the least.

What Works For Tesla

Tesla is viewed much more than an EV carmaker – it is viewed as an AI Robotics company. If the Robotaxi research and launch is successful, the impact could be remarkable (but global deployment in an era of tariff war is still a challenge in the medium term, whenever the service is ready). Tesla's high commitment to Research and Development makes it an attractive bet for the future. On the battery front, 4680 Cells and dry electrode tech research could catapult Tesla's range (already most efficient) to the next level – faster production and cheaper costs for a higher range. Investments in AI, FSD, and the Dojo Supercomputer Project are interesting and may bear fruits in a couple of years too. Tesla is also into sustainable energy ecosystem research and new product lines of significance can emerge. Potential exists in many areas – valuation follows this rationale!

All Said And Done, Tesla Is A "Hold"

Tesla's valuation is as futuristic as the company's positioning. The cutting-edge research investments can yield "who knows what when" and the impact could be multifold. Hence, the bet is not on current sales or EV, but the future. It remains speculative as of now.

Is Tesla the next Apple (AAPL)? In some ways. Tesla is heavily integrated vertically. Tesla is a cult brand like Apple with a premium perception. Only that Musk's image has taken a different trajectory than Steve Jobs' over the past few years due to his political liaison and obvious engagement. Tesla's vision is to lock in the end user to an entire ecosystem – the offering for the future is not just a product. On the flip side, Tesla is yet to be the same level of quality control as Apple from initial launches. The mass market reach is poorer yet unless the rumored $25K car hits the market.

Tesla's case based on vision and brand positioning is still strong. However, valuation has no support in such scenarios. We do not know what valuation makes sense. Since, a lot of the investments in Tesla is based on its vision and brand, it is therefore more important that the brand image is unhurt. Recent developments on brand image are a double-edged sword and may have lower than anticipated impact eventually. We don't know. But the trade war will land some trouble on Tesla, maybe lesser than some others, but there will be an impact. Global resistance – either regulatory or consumer sentiment based – will likely be a challenge.

Summarizing, vision, vertical integration, ecosystem lock-in, promising research and new product ideas are always favor Tesla's investment thesis. However, I would give it a pass as of now, expecting multiple opportunities to join the journey as more details of the promise shows results. With competition from China, recent brand and tariff impacts weighing down, it is not a great time to enter Tesla, even though it may mean missing a multi-bagger. I would have still considered a buy if the valuations were more reasonable (despite the recent fall).

The only reason, I stop short of initiating a Sell call is the recent correction. I am late by a month. Existing investors can hold Tesla without buying further on dips as of now. New investors are advised to look elsewhere, till the dust settles on tariffs and even in the long term, more developments follow Tesla's promise.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JGRW: A New Growth ETF With Relative Low Volatility
data.description:
JGRW strategy

Jensen Quality Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:JGRW) is an actively managed fund launched on 08/13/2024 currently holding 28 stocks, with an expense ratio of 0.57% and a dividend yield of 0.44%. As described by Jensen Investment Management, JGRW invests in 25 to 30 companies with “above-average potential for growth in revenue, earnings, cash flow, or other similar criteria”. These criteria include (but are not limited to) projected earnings growth, trailing revenue growth, trailing earnings growth and “the company’s ability to grow its business from free cash flow over an extended period of time”.

Eligible companies must have at least $1 billion in market capitalization, be in excellent financial condition, and show a return on equity of 15% or greater for at least ten consecutive fiscal years.

JGRW is classified by Morningstar in the “Large Blend” category. Therefore, this article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 index, represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

JGRW portfolio

The portfolio is exclusively invested in U.S. companies, mostly large and mega-caps (about 90% of asset value), with a focus on information technology (36.2%). Compared to the S&P 500, JGRW overweighs technology, industrials and healthcare, while it ignores energy, utilities and real estate.

The portfolio is very concentrated. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 57.4% of asset value, with individual weights between 4% and 8%.

Performance

JGRW has underperformed SPY by about one percentage point in total return since its inception in August 2024. Obviously, a track record of 8 months is way too short to assess the fund’s long-term potential.

JGRW vs. competitors

The next table compares characteristics of JGRW, three direct competitors implementing quality growth strategies, and one of the best performing large-cap growth ETFs:

JGRW has the highest expense ratio and the lowest AUM (assets under management) and liquidity (in dollar volume). QGRO and COWG have performed better since August 2024, although JGRW has the shallowest drawdown and lowest volatility measured in the standard deviation of the monthly return.

Takeaway

Jensen Quality Growth ETF (JGRW) is an actively managed large-cap ETF with a focus on the information technology sector. After 8 months in the market, JGRW looks much safer than other quality growth ETFs based on volatility and drawdown, but it has significantly underperformed two of them. JGRW track record is too short to assess its strategy, but investors seeking a growth fund with relative low volatility may keep it on their watch list.

data.title: Performance Food Group Company: Not Tasty Enough Amid Tariff Concerns
data.description:
One aspect of the market that, I believe, has a tendency these days to have undervalued companies is the food distribution space. These companies often trade at rather low multiples. Furthermore, while bottom-line results can and have recently been volatile, overall top-line growth is the norm. One business that operates in this space that I believe deserves some attention is Performance Food Group Company (NYSE:PFGC). In my last article about the company, published in early January of this year, I decided to reaffirm the business as a "hold" candidate.

Even though the stock looked somewhat attractive from a valuation standpoint, I was turned off by its bottom-line performance and by the fact that other similar companies were trading cheaper than it was. As disappointing as this was to see, I believed then, and I believe now that this is certainly a name that investors should continue to keep a close eye on. While I am bullish on some other players in this space right now, and while I am not currently bullish on Performance Food Group Company, it wouldn't take much for my mindset to change.

Not tasty enough yet

Before we get into the most recent data that we have regarding Performance Food Group Company, I do think a little refresher on the business is in order. At present, the company services over 300,000 customer locations in the food away-from-home industry throughout North America. Does this by the more than 150 locations that it has in operation that are dedicated to food collection and distribution? It sells a wide array of products to these enterprises, including center-of-the-plate items like beef, pork, poultry, and seafood. It sells frozen foods, groceries, candy, snacks, and beverages. Naturally, the business also sells other products like kitchen supplies and disposables. Cigarettes and tobacco products also make their way through the company's network.

Operationally speaking, the company has three different segments. Its Foodservice segment is dedicated to distributing the aforementioned products to independent and chain restaurants and other institutions. Specific examples under the other category would include schools, health care facilities, retail establishments, and more. Under its Vistar segment, the company distributes candy, snacks, and beverages, as well as products associated with office coffee service, movie theaters, hospitality providers, and more. And finally, its Convenience segment distributes candy, snacks, beverages, cigarettes, tobacco products, and more, to convenience stores across the continent.

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Performance Food Group Company covers the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During this time, revenue for the company totaled $15.64 billion. This was 9.4% above the $14.30 billion generated one year earlier. Management attributed this increase mostly to the firm's acquisition of Cheney Brothers in a deal valued at $2.1 billion. However, the firm did also see organic case volume grow by 2.1% on a year-over-year basis. Most of the organic growth was thanks to independent cases sold. Volume there jumped 5% on a year-over-year basis. Furthermore, the enterprise also benefited from product cost inflation of 4.6%, which it was able to push onto its customers.

It would be helpful to understand financial performance on a segment-by-segment basis. The big driver of the increase in revenue for the company was its Foodservice segment. Revenue there spiked 18.2% from $7.08 billion to $8.37 billion. Again, acquisition activities caused most of this. However, the company did also benefit from higher average selling prices and organic independent case volume growth of 5%. More modest was the growth associated with its other operating segments. The Vistar segment grew 2.7% from $1.20 billion to $1.23 billion. Acquisition activity helped to push this higher. And finally, the Convenience segment saw revenue growth of 0.4% from $5.94 billion to $5.97 billion. While the company did see a decline in cigarette carton sales, it did enjoy an unspecified amount of growth associated with case volume and higher product inflation that it pushed onto its customers.

The bottom line, however, was certainly disappointing. Net income actually contracted from $78.3 million to $42.4 million. This was because of a surge in operating expenses from 9.96% of sales to 10.67%. That might not seem like much of a move higher. But when you apply this to the revenue generated in just the quarter that we are talking about, that's an extra $111 million in pre-tax profits that investors are missing out on.

Management blamed this mostly on the aforementioned purchase of Cheney Brothers and the impact that it had on personnel expenses, professional fees, and more. It is worth noting that interest expense for the company also grew from $61.4 million to $100.2 million during this window of time. High interest rates and an increase in net debt can certainly be blamed for this. Other profitability metrics ended up being mixed, but largely negative. Operating cash flow plummeted from $466.9 million to $325.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a decline from $146.2 million to $130.1 million. Only EBITDA improved, rising from $345.4 million to $423 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 as a whole compared to the same time in 2024. The same exact items that contributed to the top-line improvements and the bottom-line challenges for the company for the second quarter on its own played a role in the changes for the first half of the year relative to the same time of the previous year.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, management has had some good news for investors. Previously, it was expected that revenue would come in at between $62.5 billion and $63.5 billion. That range has now been moved up to between $63 billion and $64 billion. Should the midpoint of this come to fruition, it would translate to a year-over-year increase of 9%. The bottom line has also been revised higher. Management expects, for instance, for EBITDA to be between $1.725 billion and $1.80 billion. That compares to the previous expected range of between $1.70 billion and $1.80 billion. If we assume that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate as EBITDA at the midpoint, we would get a reading for it for the year of $1.32 billion.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are multiples that are around the high end of the undervalued to the low end of the fair value range, as I typically see it. However, it is important to be cognizant of the fact that companies in this industry do have a tendency to trade at rather low multiples anyway. The low margins and volatility from a profit and cash flow perspective can be blamed for this. Compared to other similar firms, Performance Food Group Company is certainly in the fair value range. In the chart below, I compared it to five such enterprises. Using each of the two valuation metrics, I found that three of the five companies ended up being cheaper than our candidate is.

Obviously, investors at this time would be right to worry about the impact that tariffs and retaliatory tariffs might have on a company such as this. Unfortunately, we don't have a lot of insight into this. We do know that at the end of its 2024 fiscal year, the company had only four of its 144 distribution centers located in Canada, with none located in Mexico. In its latest quarterly earnings conference call, the management team at the business mentioned that they view tariffs as inflation. They also specified that they would pass those on to their customers.

Having said that, there could be disruptions in terms of how customers spend money on food. It's not unthinkable that the amount of food purchased could decline if prices rise. This is especially the case when you consider that some foods like fruits and vegetables are heavily sourced from other countries. 59% of all fresh fruit consumed in the U.S. and around 35% of all vegetables come from overseas, with much of that coming from Mexico and other Latin American countries.

There is also the fact that Performance Food Group Company has historically been focused on the food-away-from-home space. Over the past several years, this has been a good trend to bank on. As the chart above illustrates, the percentage of overall food spending in the US dedicated to food-away-from-home has risen.

As the COVID-19 pandemic revealed, however, it is not a guarantee that this trend will continue. There can be changes to it, at least in the near term. On March 25th of this year, the USDA actually came out with its own projections regarding food prices for 2025. They predicted that food-at-home prices would rise by 2.7% for the year. However, food-away-from-home prices are expected to climb by 3.7%. That analysis did not even reference the degree to which this incorporates tariffs. With many consumers already cash-strapped, and tariffs essentially serving as a tax on the consumer of the country that's importing the goods in question, it's possible that higher prices in one category could push consumers to eat more at home.

Of course, there could also be some positive effects to this when it comes to certain goods that are heavily exported from the U.S. to other nations. I wouldn't be surprised, for instance, if the decrease in demand for American corn and soybeans results in those prices dropping.

Takeaway

At this time, I still consider Performance Food Group Company to be an interesting company to be aware of and to follow. However, between the broader economic uncertainty and how the stock is priced compared to similar firms, I certainly don't think it is a prime candidate. Management's revision to revenue and profits for the year is promising. But unless we see further positive revisions and/or enjoy a decrease in the firm's share price, I think that maintaining it as a "hold" candidate makes the most sense.

data.title: Annaly Capital: Yield Curve Inverted, Time To Exit (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
NLY Stock: Yield Curve Inversion

I last analyzed Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NYSE:NLY) stock a month ago. That analysis was titled "Annaly Capital Has Broken Out (Technical Analysis)." I gave the stock a "Buy" rating at that time based on near-term technical trading patterns. More specifically, I argued that:

The spread between long- and short-term treasury rates is narrowing, a potential headwind for NLY. However, the narrowing of the yield spread is more than offset by other positive developments such as the bullish technical trading patterns and also the outlook of interest cuts in the next ~3 months.

There have been some key developments since that writing that caused me to reexamine my above thesis. First, the technical analysis was meant for the near term only. The bullish momentum at that time has run its course with the ongoing volatility in the broader market.

Second and more importantly, the market volatility has caused substantial changes in treasury rates, large enough to result in a yield curve inversion as seen in the chart below. This chart illustrates the trend of the 10-Year Treasury Rate (US10Y) and the 3-Month Treasury Rate (US3M) YTD. As seen, in January 2025, the 10-Year Treasury Rate began near 4.60% and experienced a general downward trend. As of this writing, it has declined to approximately 4.15%. In contrast, the 3-Month Treasury Rate started lower than the 10-year rates at around 4.35% in January and stayed relatively flat. The 10-year rates started to dip below the 3-month rates in late Feb. After some fluctuations, the spread has become more negative, resulting in an inverted yield curve. Currently, with the 3-month rates hovering around 4.29%, the long-short yield spread is still a negative 0.14%.

The rest of this article will analyze the potential impact of an inverted yield curve on NLY’s valuation and profits. In the end, this analysis has prompted me to downgrade my rating on NLY to sell from my earlier buy rating.

NLY Stock: Inverted Yield Curve Meets Rise Of Mortgage Delinquency

I pay special attention to the long-short treasury rates because their inversion is a key parameter for mortgage real estate investment trust, or mREIT, stocks in my model. For readers new to these stocks, their primary profit driver is the long-short yield spread. The detailed reasoning was described in my earlier article, and only the gist is quoted below. As such, the recent inversion of the yield curve serves as a key bearish sign.

… mREIT stocks in general (and NLY to a large degree) makes money on the spread between long-term and short-term rates. The thicker the long-short spread, the easier it is for mREITs to make money. The business model of mREITs is to primarily invest in mortgage-backed securities (“MBS”), which offer fixed interest rates based on the prevailing rates at the time of issuance. Wider spreads mean their longer-term MBS holdings generally offer higher interest rates compared to the shorter-term borrowings they use to finance these investments.

Additionally, another catalyst that concerns me since my last article is the persisting rise of mortgage delinquency rates. For example, the chart below shows the delinquency rate on mortgages for single-family residential. The delinquency rate was around 1.70% in the first quarter of 2024 and has been continuously climbing upward trend since then. The latest available data shows a level of 1.77% in the 4th quarter, translating into a rise of ~4% in relative terms.

Before moving, let me point out that the data here is only for the year of 2024. Later, I will put the data under a broader timeframe and show that a delinquency rate of 1.77% is not alarming under a broader scheme of things. I'm displaying the 2024 data only so that we can better see the recent trend, which is the part that concerns me, especially coupled with other economic data such as stubborn inflation, higher tariffs, and also rising delinquency rates on other consumer debts, as detailed next.

NLY Stock: Other Delinquency Rates

Other economic data further add to my concern, besides the yield curve jitter and mortgage delinquencies. For instance, the following chart displays the delinquency rate on credit card loans. As you can see, delinquency rates here have also risen sharply in 2024. The current delinquency rate is 2.97%, far above the level in the past 1 and 2 years (it was about 1.5% only in 2022). The current level is actually among the highest levels in more than a decade, as seen. Delinquency rates on other consumer debts (such as their auto loans) have also been on an upward trend lately.

The combination of these issues has made me quite concerned about the earnings outlook for NLY. I foresee consumers to start having trouble to meet their debt and mortgage obligations in the near future. I further expect an increase in mortgage delinquency rates to NLY in several ways. NLY may have to increase its provision for credit losses, which decreases its accounting EPS, with everything else being equal. If/when things get worse from here, its loan losses may increase as consumers begin to default, leading to credit losses and book value decline.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

A few other risks, both in the negative and positive directions, are worth mentioning before closing. Despite all the above issues, NLY is currently trading around a fair valuation in my view, leaving little or no margin of safety. To wit, the next figure shows NLY’s Price to Tangibles Book Value (P/TBV) in the past decade. As seen, the long-term average of its P/TBV ratio is around 1.0x, which makes perfect sense to me. The current reading is 0.97, essentially on par with the long-term average within the margin of error.

In terms of positives, NLY currently provides an above-average dividend yield as you can see from the following chart. NLY dividend yield has fluctuated significantly over the years. Notably, there was a sharp decline around 2022 due to dividend cuts. However, the yield recovered substantially, and the current value of 14.85% is too high to ignore either in relative or absolute terms. As a reference point, NLY’s average dividend yield over the past 10 years is “only” 11.83%.

To revisit a point I mentioned earlier, the current mortgage delinquency rate (1.77% if you recall) is not alarming. It is actually towards the lower end of the historical range, as you can see from the following chart. The reasons I only provided the 2024 data earlier were twofold: A) to illustrate the recent trend (the 2008 data otherwise would make the recent changes entirely invisible); and B) to make the point that it is trend that concerns me.

To wrap up, my overall assessment is that the risk/return profile for NLY stock has shifted materially since my last writing. The shift is a result of a collection of new catalysts and developments, with the inversion of the yield curve being the most crucial one in my mind. Other developments, such as the tick-up in consumer debt and mortgage delinquency rates, further compound my concerns. These issues have promoted me to downgrade its rating to sell under current conditions.

data.title: SPHY: Let's Not Forget That When Yields Go Up, So Do The Risks (For Real)
data.description:
The HY segment was one of the hardest hit in the recent downturns, and when it comes to bonds, perhaps the only one.

After all, while tariffs theoretically lead to inflation, the market is also expecting an economic slowdown, which would justify rate cuts by the Fed. This has triggered a drop in IG bond yields, widening the spread with HY.

On the flip side, HY bonds haven’t benefited from rate cut expectations, instead, they've been penalized by recession fears. It’s a unique situation, and in my view, it could likely lead to a rise in default rates, pushing spreads even wider, at least back to 2022 levels, given the similar risk dynamics.

What do I mean exactly? Let me share my take on this.

Why SPHY

SPDR® Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SPHY) is a fairly well-regarded fund in the market, even though it's not the most widely used. It offers a few features that, in my opinion, make it an interesting choice within the high-yield universe. So here’s a quick summary of my 4 key observations:

  • It invests in USD-denominated U.S. high-yield corporate bonds for at least 18 months remaining to final maturity, according to its benchmark: the ICE BofA US High Yield Index.

  • With $8.48B in AUM, it charges just 0.05% and currently has a yield to maturity of 8.50% (30-day SEC yield of 7.79%), with an option-adjusted duration of 3.13 years and average maturity of 4.63 years.

  • It holds just over 1,950 securities, mostly U.S. fixed-income corporate bonds, predominantly rated BB and B.

  • I usually analyze the HY space using another ETF, which I see as SPHY’s direct peer, USHY. They have similar durations, so it’s a good matchup. Right off the bat, SPHY has a lower AUM, meaning potentially lower liquidity, which could make it costlier for frequent trading.

But it has a lower expense ratio, which, for a buy & hold strategy, makes it more cost-effective, even factoring in the bid-ask spread.

In fact, it has delivered a higher total return.

What happened to the U.S. High Yield?

The market is pricing in a strong rate cut, starting as early as the May FOMC meeting, with a 40% probability of a Fed cut, according to the CME FedWatch Tool. Yet, the market hasn't reacted positively to this scenario.

This is a unique case, one we actually discussed here not long ago, and unfortunately, it's playing out: As recession expectations rise, the spread between HY and IG bond yields is widening.

In fact, the adjusted spread between high-yield and investment-grade yields has started to increase, triggering what we previously referred to as the “pre-crisis indicator”: the ratio between the U.S. 3-Month T-Bill yield and the HY spread, an indicator that has reliably signaled the onset of past crises.

As a result, we've seen a sharp drop in HY bonds and a rally in investment-grade debt.

Is this an opportunity?

That’s the question we’re all asking:

Is it time to buy the dip and lock in a +7% yield during this phase of “everything dump”?

Personally, I see 3 key risk factors that could lead to a further widening of the spread:

    • Declining corporate revenues: Due to the introduction of tariffs, which could reduce earnings, lead to downward revisions in EPS growth expectations, and increase recessionary pressures. The first earnings guidance for Q1 2025 is already predominantly negative.

  • Debt maturity & rising financing costs: A massive wave of corporate debt will need to be refinanced in 2025–2026. That could lead to higher funding costs, especially for small caps or lower-quality corporates included in this ETF (I’ve covered this in detail here). Think about it: Isn’t it odd that, after one of the fastest rate hikes in history, the HY–IG spread had narrowed to near historical lows? One reason is that many companies refinanced during the post-COVID era at near-zero rates, but those loans are coming due, and pressure will build starting 2025–2026.

  • Capital flight toward cash and cash-like alternatives: A shift toward T-Bills and short-duration TIPS is already underway, that means lower demand for high yield, which could further widen spreads.

My opinion

I don’t think it’s a coincidence that the worst-performing segments in the market right now are small caps (via the Russell 2000) and high-yield bonds. It’s a strange dynamic because if the market’s focus were truly shifting toward protectionism, these segments wouldn’t necessarily be the most negatively affected.

In fact, during Trump’s first election cycle, when tariff talks were front and center, these exact categories saw strong gains. Now, however, the market is pricing in the exact opposite scenario.

That’s why I, personally, expect a rise in HY default rates, and I’d rather wait for wider spreads, at least at the levels seen in 2022, especially since the perceived risk today is quite similar.

Risk

Of course, taking this stance could involve a significant opportunity cost.

The recent drop in HY has pushed prices into a statistically attractive zone, which in the past has often marked a solid entry point.

Applying the 14-period RSI on a 1-week timeframe, we see that prices have now hit the 30-level lows, a typical oversold signal. And with a yield above 7%, this setup becomes particularly appealing as a potential opportunity.

Conclusion

Personally, I prefer to be patient and make my entry based on yield spreads rather than charts, which, in the end, are mostly a reflection of those spreads.

I’m sticking with a HOLD rating, precisely because I see this as more of a mid-zone, somewhere between a true bottom and the highs (which coincided with minimum spread levels).

data.title: Voya Strategic Income Opportunities Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Unconstrained and flexible approach, investing broadly across the global debt markets.

Key takeaways

• Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24.

• The Fund underperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA USD 3M Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity Index (the Index), on net asset value basis.

• Sector allocation along with security selection decisions contributed over the quarter while duration and yield curve decisions detracted over the period, where the rate rally seen in the previous quarter reversed course as inflation concerns and a more cautious U.S. Federal Reserve led to markets repricing yields higher.

• Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation.

Portfolio review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24. The gross domestic product (GDP) report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explains both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed's Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

Sector allocation along with security selection decisions contributed over the quarter while duration and yield curve decisions detracted over the period, where the rate rally seen in the previous quarter reversed course as inflation concerns and a more cautious Fed led to markets repricing yields higher. Our overweight to securitized credit contributed broadly over the period. The non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and credit risk transfers (CRT) sector was the largest contributor, where spreads tightened on positive economic fundamental factors and optimism for future policies. Allocation to corporate credit also contributed, albeit to a lesser extent.

Additionally, positive security selection results in agency mortgages were realized due to our allocation to off-benchmark collateralized mortgage obligations (CMO) which outperformed despite the underperformance of Index constituents.

Current strategy and outlook

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit sectors, however we continue to favor high-quality, shorter-dated assets given where valuations are. Over the quarter we reduced our allocation to longer dated investment grade corporates and are now positioned with an underweight from a spread duration perspective.

Similarly, we maintain only a modest allocation to high yield corporates, while high quality securitized makes up much of our active risk. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Voya Multi-Manager International Small Cap Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Invests primarily in securities of small market capitalization companies located outside the United States, including emerging markets.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the U.S. Federal Reserve’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Portfolio review

The Fund’s manager target allocations as of December 31, 2024, were 50% to Victory Capital Management, LLC and 50% to Acadian Asset Management, LLC.

Sub-adviser details

Acadian Asset Management, LLC

For the quarter, the Fund’s Acadian Asset Management, LLC (AAMI) sleeve outperformed the S&P Developed ex-U.S. Small Cap Index. Stock selection and country allocations contributed to returns.

Key sources of positive active return included stock selection in both Canada and Japan, and a combination of stock selection and an underweight position in the United Kingdom. Leading advances within these markets respectively included a position in CI Financial Corp., a holding in Konica Minolta Inc., and an investment in JET2 Plc. Detractors included a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in Norway, and stock selection in Denmark and an overweight position in Brazil. Leading declines within these markets in turn included a position in Hoegh Autoliners ASA, a holding in Rockwool AS and an investment in C&AModas S.A.

From a sector perspective, key sources of positive active return included a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in information technology, a combination of stock selection and an underweight position in materials, as well as stock selection in consumer discretionary. Leading advances within these sectors respectively included a position in SimilarWeb Ltd., a holding in Regis Resources Ltd., and an investment in Pandora AS. Lastly, a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in industrials detracted from active return led by a position in Rockwool AS.

Bottom-up stock selection continued to drive the portfolio.

Victory Capital Management, LLC

For the quarter, the Fund’s Victory Capital Management, LLC sleeve outperformed the S&P Developed ex-U.S. Small Cap Index. Security selection was positive as excess returns were generated in three of five regions and seven of the eleven economic sectors. From a style perspective, the Fund’s overall exposure to value, business momentum and quality factors contributed to relative performance, with business momentum generating the strongest signal. At the sector level, notable outperformance was generated in information technology and consumer discretionary. Information technology performance was boosted by a sizable position in Celestica, Inc. which remains a top contributor for the year following stronger-than-expected quarterly results and upgraded guidance. The Canadian electronics manufacturing company remains a beneficiary of hyperscaler spending on data center equipment. Technology One Ltd. was another strong performer. The Australian business software company gained after reporting full-year results which included a steady increase in annual recurring revenue. Within consumer discretionary, Round One Corp., a Japanese operator of indoor leisure facilities, moved higher with positive monthly sales growth alongside continued store expansion optimism in the United States.

On the downside, selection was weakest in materials, with the largest impact coming from two holdings. Swiss chemical company Clariant AG fell with other producers as the demand environment continues to be difficult and restocking expectations have been pushed to later in 2025. Korean defense and copper producer Poongsan Corp. declined as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East appeared to ease and base metals prices experienced a downturn during the quarter. Relative performance also lagged in utilities and was driven down by a position in Boralex Inc., a Canadian renewable energy company with 24% of its capacity in the U.S. The stock declined on concerns of a weaker growth outlook for U.S. renewable energy.

We continue to be guided by our bottom-up analysis and remain focused on stock selection while adhering to our disciplined country and sector risk exposures.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report—percentage of portfolio investments, as of 12/31/2024: CI Financial Corp. (OTCPK:CIXXF) 0.00%, Konica Minolta, Inc. (OTCPK:KNCAF) 0.12%, JET2 Plc. (OTCPK:DRTGF) 0.64%, Hoegh Autoliners ASA 0.05%, Rockwool AS (OTCPK:RKWAD) 0.88%, C&AModas S.A. 0.00%, SimilarWeb Ltd. (SMWB) 0.16%, Regis Resources Ltd. (OTCPK:RGRNF) 0.20%, Pandora AS (OTCPK:PNDZF) 1.35%, Celestica, Inc. (CLS) 0.85%, Technology One Ltd. (OTCPK:THNOF) 0.39%, Round One Corp. (OTCPK:RNDOF) 0.31%, Clariant AG (OTCPK:CLZNF) 0.24%, Poongsan Corp. 0.02%, and Boralex Inc. (OTCPK:BRLXF) 0.16%.

0.00% indicates that the security was not or is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to daily change.

The S&P Developed Ex-U.S. SmallCap Index is an unmanaged index of small-cap stocks from developed countries, excluding the United States. The Index does not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

The MSCI Europe, Australasia and Far East (EAFE) Small Cap Index is an unmanaged index which measures the performance of small capitalization equities among developed markets around the world, excluding the United States and Canada. The Index does not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. You could lose money on your investment and any of the following risks, among others, could affect investment performance. The following principal risks are presented in alphabetical order which does not imply order of importance or likelihood: China Investing Risks – Investing through Stock Connect; Company; Convertible Securities; Credit; Currency; Derivative Instruments; Environmental, Social, and Governance (Equity); Environmental, Social, and Governance (Multi-Manager); Environmental, Social, and Governance (Qualitative); Foreign (Non-U.S.) Investments/ Developing and Emerging Markets; Index Strategy (Fund); Growth Investing; Interest Rate; Investment Model; Liquidity; Market; Market Disruption and Geopolitical; Other Investment Companies; Real Estate Companies and Real Estate Investment Trusts; Securities Lending; Small-Capitalization Company; Value Investing. Investors should consult the Fund’s Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund’s risks.

An investment in the Fund is not a bank deposit and is not insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the Federal Reserve Board or any other government agency.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management’s current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors.

The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.

©2024 Voya Investments Distributor, LLC • 230 Park Ave, New York, NY 10169 • All rights reserved.
